title: Unity game engine basic identity
content: Unity is a game engine used to build video games and interactive 3D or 2D applications. It provides tools for scenes, objects, scripting, physics, and exporting projects to different platforms. Belle should distinguish Unity the engine from Unity Technologies or Unity Software the company.

title: Unity is not only for games
content: Unity is strongly associated with game development, but it can also be used for simulations, training tools, visualization, and interactive experiences. For beginner answers, it is safe to call it a game engine while noting that it supports other real-time interactive projects too.

title: Unity scenes and GameObjects concept
content: In Unity, a project is commonly built from scenes that contain GameObjects. GameObjects can represent characters, cameras, lights, items, or invisible managers. This simple mental model helps beginners understand how a Unity game level or screen is organized.

title: C# scripting in Unity
content: Unity commonly uses C# scripting to define game behavior. Scripts can control movement, input, collisions, UI, and gameplay rules. A beginner-friendly answer should explain that Unity handles the engine side while C# scripts tell objects what to do.

title: Unity cross-platform context
content: Unity is known for supporting deployment to multiple platforms, depending on project settings, licenses, and target requirements. This is one reason it is popular with many developers. Belle should avoid promising that every Unity project can be exported everywhere without extra work.

title: Unity 2D and 3D support
content: Unity can be used for both 2D and 3D games. A 2D project may use sprites, tilemaps, and 2D physics, while a 3D project may use meshes, cameras, lighting, and 3D physics. The engine supports both styles inside the same broader toolset.

title: Unity engine versus Unity Editor
content: The Unity engine is the runtime and technology that powers projects, while the Unity Editor is the tool developers use to build scenes, import assets, and configure behavior. Beginners often say Unity for both. Belle can clarify the difference only when needed.

title: Unity physics beginner context
content: Unity includes physics systems that can help simulate movement, collisions, gravity, and rigid bodies. Developers still need to configure objects, colliders, and scripts correctly. A simple answer should avoid implying that realistic physics works automatically without setup.

title: Unity asset workflow context
content: Unity projects use assets such as models, textures, audio, scripts, animations, and prefabs. The editor helps organize and place these assets into scenes. For a beginner, assets are the building blocks that become visible or functional parts of the game.

title: Unity Technologies company identity
content: Unity Technologies, later associated with Unity Software, is the company behind the Unity engine. A search answer should separate the company from the engine itself. Users asking about installing, licensing, or company history may need the company context rather than gameplay concepts.

title: Unity license and pricing caution
content: Unity licensing and pricing can change over time, so a static knowledge base should not give fixed current prices or legal advice. Belle can explain that Unity has licensing terms and recommend checking official Unity information for current plans, fees, and conditions.

title: Unity as a learning engine
content: Unity is often used by beginners because it combines visual editing with C# scripting and many learning resources. It can teach general game development ideas such as scenes, objects, components, input, and physics. Belle should still explain that learning Unity takes practice, not just installing the editor.

title: Role-playing video game basic definition
content: A role-playing video game is a game where players usually control one or more characters, improve abilities, and progress through quests, battles, or story choices. RPGs can be turn-based, action-based, single-player, or online. Belle should explain the genre broadly instead of forcing one fixed formula.

title: Character progression in RPGs
content: Character progression is a common RPG idea where characters become stronger or more specialized over time. This may involve levels, skills, equipment, stats, or story upgrades. The exact system depends on the game, so Belle should describe the idea without assuming every RPG uses the same mechanics.

title: Party system in RPGs
content: Many RPGs use a party system, where the player manages several characters instead of only one. Party members may have different roles, elements, weapons, or abilities. This concept helps explain team-building in games like Honkai: Star Rail without claiming all RPGs work the same way.

title: Quests and story in RPGs
content: RPGs often use quests to guide players through story, exploration, combat, or character development. A quest can be a main story task or an optional side activity. Belle can explain quests as structured goals that give players direction inside a game world.

title: Stats in RPGs
content: Stats in RPGs are numbers that describe character abilities, such as health, attack, defense, speed, or special power. Different games choose different stats and formulas. A beginner answer should describe stats as gameplay values, not as real-world measurements of a character.

title: Action RPG versus turn-based RPG
content: An action RPG usually asks players to control timing and movement in real time, while a turn-based RPG separates actions into turns. Both can still be RPGs because they may include character growth, parties, quests, and story. Belle can use this difference for ZZZ, HI3, and HSR comparisons.

title: Single-player video game basic definition
content: A single-player video game is designed so one person can play the main experience alone. It may still have online updates, accounts, or optional features, but the core play does not require other human teammates. Belle should not confuse single-player with offline-only.

title: Story focus in single-player games
content: Single-player games often focus on story, exploration, puzzles, or personal progression because the experience can be paced around one player. This is not required for every single-player game, but it is common. Belle can explain the appeal without saying multiplayer games cannot have story.

title: Single-player does not always mean offline
content: Single-player means the main gameplay is for one player, but it does not always mean the game works fully offline. Some single-player or mostly solo games still use online accounts, downloads, events, or cloud saves. Belle should separate player count from internet requirements.

title: Gacha random reward system
content: A gacha game uses a random reward system to obtain characters, items, or equipment. Players usually spend in-game currency, earned resources, or real money for pulls. A neutral answer should explain randomness and avoid encouraging spending or promising lucky results.

title: Banner caution in gacha games
content: In many gacha games, banners are time-limited or rotating offers for certain rewards. Because banners change, a static knowledge base should not claim what is available right now. Belle should use fresh official information when users ask about current banners or dates.

title: Pity system neutral explanation
content: Some gacha games use pity or guarantee systems that change the chance or certainty of receiving a reward after many pulls. Rules differ by game and can change. Belle should explain the concept generally and tell users to check the specific game's official details.

title: Spending safety in gacha games
content: Gacha systems can involve real money, so answers should stay neutral and careful. Belle should not pressure users to spend, chase a character, or believe a lucky streak is guaranteed. A safer response encourages budgeting, reading rates, and treating pulls as random entertainment.

title: Rates and probability in gacha games
content: Gacha rewards are based on probability, so rare results may not appear even after many attempts unless a game has a guarantee rule. Published rates differ between games and banners. Belle should avoid inventing rates and should not say a result is due soon.

title: Spiritual successor basic definition
content: A spiritual successor is a work that feels related to an earlier work through style, themes, ideas, or design, even if it is not a direct sequel. The relationship should be supported by sources or clear creator statements. Belle should not label games this way only because fans feel they are similar.
aliases: game giống nhưng không phải phần tiếp

title: Spiritual successor is not a direct sequel
content: A direct sequel continues a previous work more explicitly, while a spiritual successor mainly carries a similar spirit or design approach. It may have different characters, setting, or ownership. This distinction helps Belle avoid saying one game is officially connected when it is only similar.

title: Game genre search for RAG
content: Game genre terms such as RPG, action RPG, single-player, and gacha are useful search keys in a RAG system. Users often ask by genre before they remember a title. Belle should retrieve genre objects for definitions and title objects for specific game facts.

title: Live-service general caution
content: Live-service games can change through updates, seasons, patches, and events. A static knowledge file is good for stable definitions and background facts, but it should not be treated as current patch data. Belle should use fresh sources for schedules, rewards, and balance changes.
